coredata.dc:title: Enhanced degradation performance and mineralization of ciprofloxacin by ionizing radiation combined with g-C3N4/CDs
coredata.dc:creator: He, Junhui
Ye, Qi
Zhu, Yi
Yang, Miao
Zhao, Long
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was investigated in the synergetic system of electron beam irradiation (EBI) combined with graphite carbon nitride/carbon nanodots (g-C3N4/CDs). The results suggested that the presence of g-C3N4/CDs could not only promote the degradation of CIP but also significantly enhanced the mineralization ratio. At 25 kGy, the mineralization ratio increased from 18.1% to 69.0% while g-C3N4/CDs was added. Regeneration experiments revealed that the g-C3N4/CDs composite possessed good reusability. In addition to finding that the presence of CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 − and NO2 − obviously restrained the radiolysis process of CIP, the adding of Cl− and SO4 2− could also have slight inhibitory effects. The effect of various water matrices in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems suggested that the degradation efficiency decreased in lake and tap water. Moreover, the studies in the presence of different radical scavengers suggested that ·OH was the main reactive species in the degradation process. The possible degradation pathway of CIP was proposed in the light of intermediate products using Liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC–MS) analysis coupled with DFT calculations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ciprofloxacin
Heterogeneous irradiation system
G-C3N4/CDs
Electron beam irradiation
Degradation mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Radiolysis kinetic studies of CIP under EBI without g-C3N4/CDs
Radiolysis studies with various radical scavengers
The DFT calculation and degradation pathway of CIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Electromagnetic Engineering and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Electromagnetic Engineering and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Advanced Electromagnetic Engineering and Technology (Grant No. AEET 2022KF011 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21707108 ). The authors were grateful to the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for providing the facilities to fulfill the experimental measurements. The authors would like to thank the radiation chemistry and functional materials research group of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for the EBI support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR and (b) XRD spectra of g-C3N4/CDs composite before and after EBI.
Effect of different initial concentrations on (a) CIP degradation and (b) G-values in single EBI systems.
(a) Effect of g-C3N4/CDs dosage on CIP degradation in the EBI&g-C3N4/CDs system, (b) mineralization ratio of single EBI and EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems, m[g-C3N4/CDs]/V(CIP) = 8 mgmL−1, [CIP]0 = 200 mgL−1
Regeneration test on CIP removal at 25 kGy, m[g-C3N4/CDs]/V(CIP) = 8 mgmL−1, [CIP]0 = 200 mgL−1

Effect of different anions on the degradation of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems, m[g-C3N4/CDs]/V(CIP) = 8 mgmL−1, [CIP]0 = 200 mgL−1, [Cl−] = 0.1 M, [SO4 2−] = 0.05 M, [CO3 2−] = 0.05 M, [HCO3 −] = 0.05 M, [NO3 −] = 0.05 M, [NO2 −] = 0.05 M.
Effect of different water matrices on CIP degradation in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems, m[g-C3N4/CDs]/V(CIP) = 8 mgmL−1, [CIP]0 = 200 mgL−1
Radiolysis of CIP in four different radical scavenger EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems, m[g-C3N4/CDs]/V(CIP) = 8 mgmL−1, [CIP]0 = 200 mgL−1
(a) The HOMO and LUMO of CIP; (b) charge distribution and Fukui index of CIP; (c) proposed degradation pathway of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs synergistic system.
Degradation kinetic parameters of CIP with different initial concentrations under EBI.
The main intermediates detected by LC-MS during CIP radiolysis process in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs synergistic system.

head.ce:title: Enhanced degradation performance and mineralization of ciprofloxacin by ionizing radiation combined with g-C3N4/CDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Electromagnetic Engineering and Technology, School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, China

School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Life Sciences, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) was investigated in the synergetic system of electron beam irradiation (EBI) combined with graphite carbon nitride/carbon nanodots (g-C3N4/CDs). The results suggested that the presence of g-C3N4/CDs could not only promote the degradation of CIP but also significantly enhanced the mineralization ratio. At 25 kGy, the mineralization ratio increased from 18.1% to 69.0% while g-C3N4/CDs was added. Regeneration experiments revealed that the g-C3N4/CDs composite possessed good reusability. In addition to finding that the presence of CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 − and NO2 − obviously restrained the radiolysis process of CIP, the adding of Cl− and SO4 2− could also have slight inhibitory effects. The effect of various water matrices in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems suggested that the degradation efficiency decreased in lake and tap water. Moreover, the studies in the presence of different radical scavengers suggested that ·OH was the main reactive species in the degradation process. The possible degradation pathway of CIP was proposed in the light of intermediate products using Liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC–MS) analysis coupled with DFT calculations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The required documents have been uploaded

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of penicillin, antibiotics have been widely applied to different fields, such as medical treatment, livestock farming and aquaculture (Landers et al., 2012; Castanon, 2007). Antibiotics released to the environment inevitably contaminate soil and water sources and eventually have hazardous effect on human body (Alsager et al., 2018). Due to the extensive use of antibiotics and their environmental persistence, antibiotics accumulated in environment gradually become emerging pollutants. It has been reported that antibiotics have been detected in surface water, groundwater, seawater and soil in different regions (Bilal et al., 2020). The removal of antibiotics has become one of the important challenges in the environmental field. Among antibiotic compounds, ciprofloxacin (CIP) is the most common fluoroquinolone antibiotic and the presence of the fluorine atom in its structure results in its resistance and environmental sustainability (Jiang et al., 2018; Malakootian et al., 2019). Various methods have been applied to remove CIP, such as ultrasound, adsorption, photocatalysis, UV etc. (Sutar and Rathod, 2015; Gadipelly et al., 2018; Lacerda et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2013)

Recently, ionizing irradiation, including gamma irradiation and electron beam irradiation (EBI), has been used for wastewater treatment due to its advantages of simple process, high efficiency, and no secondary pollution. In general, reductive (eaq − and ·H) and oxidative (·OH) free radicals can be produced simultaneously during radiolysis process of water (Liu et al., 2016). The target contaminants could be decomposed by both·OH oxidation and eaq − reduction (Selambakkannu et al., 2020). Homogeneous ionizing radiation is an effective method to degrade pollutants, but in some cases, the mineralization efficiency is not high (Chu et al., 2018; H. Zhang et al., 2016). Compared with homogeneous ionization irradiation, there are few studies on heterogeneous ionization irradiation technology at present. Ionization irradiation is combined with other non-homogeneous catalysts, such as TiO2 (Yang et al., 2007), g-C3N4 (Ma et al., 2022) and Fenton-like catalysts (Chen et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Zhuan and Wang, 2020), has the advantages of improving degradation and mineralization effects and reducing energy consumption. Thus, it's very necessary to combine irradiation techniques with other processes.

g-C3N4/CDs is a kind of composite with cheap raw materials, simple preparation method and high specific surface area that can absorb target contaminants and concentrate. Furthermore, CDs possess excellent catalytic activity, which can catalyze hydrogen peroxide generated during the radiation process to form ·OH (Xu et al., 2012). It has been proved in our previous work that graphite carbon nitride/carbon nanodots (g-C3N4/CDs) exhibited good performance when combined with EBI to remove and mineralize methylene blue (Ye et al., 2022). Theoretically, g-C3N4/CDs composite has a synergistic effect with EBI in the process of contaminant treatment.

In this study, the synergistic performance between g-C3N4/CDs and EBI on the removal of CIP was evaluated in detail. Considering the ubiquitous solutes in natural water, the effects of co-existing anions and different water matrices on degradation were also investigated in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems. And the possible degradation pathway was proposed by LC-MS analysis and DFT calculation.

Ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CIP, 98%), formic acid (AR, 85%) and NaHCO3 (AR) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl (AR), NaSO4 (AR), Na2CO3 (AR), NaNO3 (AR), NaNO2 (AR), isopropanol (IPA, AR) and acetonitrile used for HPLC analysis were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Urea (AR) and citric acid monohydrate (AR) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals above were used without further purification. The lake water used in water matrices experiment was obtained from East Lake located in Wuhan, Hubei Province. Except for water matrices experiment, all solutions were prepared by deionized water.

The g-C3N4/CDs composite was synthesized based on the previous report with a slight modification (Fang et al., 2019). In thermal polymerization step, the mixture of citric acid and urea was calcined in a muffle furnace at a ramping rate of 1 °C min−1 and then kept for 3h at 550 °C. After natural cooling to room temperature, the prepared g-C3N4/CDs composite was collected, washed, dried and ground.

All irradiation processes were carried using a 1.0 MeV electron accelerator (dose per pass: 5 kGy/pass) in the School of Hubei University of Science and Technology. In the irradiation process, the speed of the trolley under the beam is 6m/min, and the trolley runs for about 6min once. The dose was measured by FWT-60-00 film color developer dose tablet. In EBI&g-C3N4/CDs system, the g-C3N4/CDs composite was added into the CIP solution and mixed before irradiation. All irradiation treatments were conducted in triplicate at room temperature, and uncertainties percentages are 3% or less.

Brunauer–Emmet–Teller (BET) method was performed to determine the specific surface area and porosity of synthesized g-C3N4/CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to analyze the surface group of g-C3N4/CDs composite with a range of 500–4000 cm−1. The morphology was recorded by using transmission electron microscope (TEM). Samples for the TEM were prepared by ultrasonically dispersing the powders in ethanol, and then droplets were placed on carbon-coated Cu grids. The crystal structure and phase were analyzed by an X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) over a 2θrange of 5–70° at room temperature.

The concentration of CIP before and after irradiation was determined using HPLC (UltiMate 3000, Thermofisher, USA). The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid (30:70, v/v) with a flow rate of 1.0mLmin−1. The injected volume was 20 μL and the detection wavelength was 277 nm. The chromatographic separation was performed using C18 reverse phase column (5 μm, 4.8
×
250 mm) and the retention time was 3.4min. The temperature of the column was set at 30 °C. The intermediates of CIP degradation were determined by LC-MS with ESI ion operating in the positive mode. Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured by a TOC analyzer (Elementar, Germany) to reflect the mineralization of CIP after EBI.

All the density functional theory (DFT) computations were performed on Gaussian 09 program package in this study. The mechanism and degradation pathway of CIP was elucidated based on DFT calculation using the Gaussian 09 software package and Multiwfn (Lu and Chen, 2012). Geometrical and electronic structures of the CIP molecule were optimized by DFT using the B3LYP method at 6-31G(d) basis set level in a water solvent.

The FT-IR analysis of g-C3N4/CDs was shown in Fig. 1 a (The spectra was offset for viewing). The broad band at 3166 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of N−H (Chen et al., 2018; Tonda et al., 2014). The peak at 2170 cm−1 was allocated to the CN bonds (Zhu et al., 2014). The characteristic peak in the region of 1200–1600 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of the CN heterocycle and the peak at 820 cm−1 was allocated to the stretching vibrations of triazine units (Xu et al., 2014; Z. Zhang et al., 2016). It could be seen that the above-mentioned characteristic peaks of g-C3N4/CDs remained unchanged after EBI. While the peaks at 2925 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 newly appeared in the spectra of irradiated g-C3N4/CDs, which was attributed to the stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2. This phenomenon could be explained by the attached CIP or intermediates that were not washed out during the process of collecting the material. The crystal structural of g-C3N4/CDs was characterized by XRD patterns (Fig. 1b). The {002} diffraction peak at 27.3° and {001} peak at 13.4° were observed, corresponding to the interlaminar stacking of aromatic structures and in-plane structural repeat units of triazine, respectively (Wu et al., 2020). NO peaks related to CDs were found, which ascribed to the low amount of CDs (Asadzadeh-Khaneghah et al., 2021). No changes appeared in the XRD peaks after irradiation, which confirmed the stability of the g-C3N4/CDs composite. The morphology of g-C3N4/CDs was assayed through TEM analysis (Fig. S1). As illustrated in Fig. S1, CDs were successfully deposited on layered g-C3N4. From the nitrogen adsorption/desorption curves (Fig. S2a), the specific surface area of g-C3N4/CDs was calculated to be 71.10 ± 0.25m2g−1. It was observed from Barrett–Joyner–Halenda pore-size distributions (Fig. S2b) that the pore sizes were mainly distributed below 20 nm and the average pore diameter was calculated to be 10.91 nm. From above results, it could be seen that the porous g-C3N4/CDs was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiolysis kinetic studies of CIP under EBI without g-C3N4/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different initial concentrations on CIP decomposition using EBI without g-C3N4/CDs was exhibited in Fig. 2 a. The degradation efficiency of CIP (50 mgL−1) was 94.5% with the irradiation dose of 5 kGy, and which reached 99.7 with 10 kGy, suggesting the EBI approach was more efficient than some non-ionizing radiation methods (Liu et al., 2022; Shah et al., 2020). With the initial concentrations of 100, 150 and 200 mgL−1, the degradation efficiency of CIP was 88.8, 81.7 and 63.8 at 5 kGy, respectively. The degradation efficiency of CIP increased with irradiation dose increased and initial concentration decreased. Similar results could be found in electron beam induced degradation of pesticides, drugs and antibiotics (Changotra et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015).

The radiolysis of CIP by EBI could be described by the following pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics (Eq. (1)) (Silva et al., 2016). D0.5 and D0.9 are used to calculate the dose required to degrade 50% and 90% of the initial CIP concentration (Eq. (2) and (3)).
(1)
− ln
(
C
C 0
)
=
(2)
D 0.5
=
ln
( 2 )
k
(3)
D 0.9
=
ln
( 10 )
k
where
C 0
is CIP concentration before EBI (mg L−1),
C
is CIP concentration after EBI (mg L−1), k is dose constant (kGy−1), and D is the radiation dose (kGy).

Radiation chemical yield (G-value) could be used to describe the irradiation efficiency, which is calculated by Eq. (4).
(4)
G =
Δ R
N A
6.24 ×
10 19
D
In which
Δ R
is the variation amount of the target pollutant (mol L−1); NA is
6.02 ×
10 23
(Avogadro constant); D is the radiation dose (kGy);
6.24 ×
10 19
is the conversion factor from kGy to 100 eVL−1. G-value is expressed in
μ m o l
J−1, considering 1 molecules
×
(100 eV)−1 = 0.10364
μ m o l
J
−1.

Fig. 2b illustrated the G-values of CIP at different CIP initial concentrations and irradiation doses. It can be seen that the G-value decreased as irradiation dose increased from 0 to 25 kGy at a certain CIP initial concentration, which was consistent with previous reports (Changotra et al., 2020; Zhuan and Wang, 2019). The probable reason of reduced G-value was ascribed to the increase of radical–radical recombination reactions when irradiation doses raised, leading to less ·OH available for participating in the reactions with contaminants (Sánchez-Polo et al., 2009). Furthermore, the augment of the G-value when initial CIP concentration increased could be explained by more CIP molecules exposed to reactive species.

Fig. 3 a depicted the effect of g-C3N4/CDs dosage on CIP decomposition under EBI. It was observed that the degradation efficiency of CIP increased as g-C3N4/CDs dosage raised. After 5 kGy irradiation, the degradation efficiency was enhanced from 63.8% to 72.8% and 77.4% with the g-C3N4/CDs dosage of 4 mgmL−1 and 8 mgmL−1, respectively. The dose constants of radiolysis process were fitted by the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation. Compared with 0.14 kGy-1 of the control group, the dose constants were raised in the presence of g-C3N4/CDs, which were calculated 0.17 kGy-1 with the dosage of 8 mgmL−1. Without irradiation, g-C3N4 can achieve partial adsorption of CIP, it was worth mentioning that the initial concentrations of irradiation were different after adsorption. However, when taking this as the benchmark, the degradation efficiency of EBI&g-C3N4/CDs system with the dosage of 8 mgmL−1 was 67.4% at 5 kGy, which was still higher than that of the control group (63.8%).

As shown in Fig. 3b, it was notable that g-C3N4/CDs addition could improve the antibiotic mineralization. EBI was an efficient method for the degradation of CIP. However, its mineralization was mediocre (Chen et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2007, 2020; Zhuan and Wang, 2020). When g-C3N4/CDs was added, the TOC degradation efficiency increased from 18.1% to 69.0% at 25 kGy, which was raised by nearly 4 times. It can be seen from 0 kGy that the mineralization contributed by the adsorption process was only 34.6%, but in the presence of EBI, the TOC removal ratio of nearly 50% was still achieved at 25 kGy based on the remaining TOC value after adsorption. The results above indicated that EBI is necessary, and the synergetic system of EBI combined with g-C3N4/CDs could not only promote the degradation of CIP but also significantly enhanced the mineralization ratio.

To investigate the reusability of g-C3N4/CDs composite, the removal of CIP at 25 kGy with initial concentration of 200 mgL−1 was conducted over five cycles. The material was washed by deionized water and collected after irradiation applied. The obtained powders were utilized for next run. It's noted that the degradation efficiency of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs synergetic systems exceeded 98% within all five cycles (Fig. 4 ), which suggested that the g-C3N4/CDs composite possessed good reusability (Ye et al., 2022).

It was very necessary to discuss the effect of co-existing ions on CIP radiolysis process since there were many inorganic anions in actual water, which might affect the decomposition of CIP. Fig. 5 demonstrated the influence of Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 − and NO2 − on the degradation of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs synergetic systems. The addition of CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 − and NO2 − restrained the radiolysis process of CIP, of which the suppression effect of NO2 − was most obvious and CO3 2− ranked the second. The phenomenon could be explained by the high rate constant of NO2 − and CO3 2− reacting with ·OH, which were 6.0 × 109 Lmol−1 s−1 and 3.9 × 108 Lmol−1 s−1, respectively (Eqs. (5)–(7)) (Shah et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). Besides, HCO3 − could combine with ·OH and eaq −, while NO3 − could scavenge eaq − (Eq. (8-10)) (Guo et al., 2017; Shah et al., 2014). The presence of Cl− and SO4 2− slightly inhibited the decomposition of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems. This was because the reaction rate constants of SO4 2− with ·OH and eaq − were relatively low (Eq. (11-12)) (Chu et al., 2019). And although Cl− could scavenge ·OH with rate constant of 4.3 × 109 Lmol−1 s−1, the produced ClOH· may decompose to form Cl− and OH· (k = 6.1 × 109 Lmol−1 s−1) (Chu et al., 2019). Based on the analysis above, it could be inferred that ·OH might play a vital role during the radiolysis process of CIP in the heterogeneous irradiation system (Chu et al., 2019; Shah et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2018).
(5)

In order to investigate the practical application, the removal of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems was estimated in three different water matrices. As shown in Fig. 6 , before radiation, it's worth mentioning that there were different adsorption effects in three water matrices. The phenomenon might be related to the co-existing inorganic anions and natural organic matter in real water matrices. In radiolysis process, the degradation efficiency decreased both in tap water and lake water. The dose constant reached 0.10 kGy-1 in lake water system, which was lower than tap water and deionized water with dose constants of 0.13 kGy-1 and 0.17 kGy-1, respectively. It could be explained by the solutes in the water matrices, which might compete with the target pollutants for the reactive species (Zhuan and Wang, 2020). Moreover, there were more solutes in lake water than tap water, leading to the lower rate constant of the former. Nevertheless, there was still a valid degradation effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiolysis studies with various radical scavengers
ce:section.ce:para:
As mentioned above, ·OH may play the vital role in the degradation process of CIP. To further explicate the role of three reactive species in the radiolysis process, four different oxidation–reduction systems were executed with a variety of specific radical scavengers including N2 saturated, O2 saturated and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) (0.1 and 0.5 M) with N2 saturated systems. IPA was a good scavenger for ·OH (
k = 1.9 ×
10 9
L
m o l
− 1
S
− 1
) and ·H (
k = 7.4 ×
10 7
L
m o l
− 1
S
− 1
), which made eaq − the main reactive specie in IPA/N2 saturated system (Eq. (13-14)) (Shah et al., 2015b). In O2 saturated system, two kinds of reductive radicals, eaq − and ·H were captured with rate constants of
9.1 ×
10 10
L
m o l
− 1
S
− 1
and
2.1 ×
10 10
L
m o l
− 1
S
− 1
, respectively (Eq. (15-16)). Weak reactive radicals
O 2
• −
and
H O
2 •
were subsequently generated and ·OH was the main reactive radical of O2 saturated system (Chen and Wang, 2020). It can be observed in Fig. 7 that the rate of O2 saturated system approximated to that of N2 saturated system, which indicated the vital role of ·OH in the heterogeneous irradiation processes. The presence of IPA inhibited the decomposition of CIP and no significant variations were observed between two experimental groups with different concentrations, suggesting both ·OH and ·H play important part in the radiolysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The DFT calculation and degradation pathway of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, two CIP degradation pathways were proposed and are shown in Fig. 8. (c). In pathway Ⅰ, the piperazine ring of CIP could be attacked by OH· to produce amide P1 (m/z 379). P1 then decarbonylated and subsequently decomposed into the smaller product of P2 (m/z 308) by losing “CH2CH2NH2” group (F. Chen et al., 2020). Followed by the “-CO” group loss, the P3 (m/z 280) was generated. The P3 decarboxylated to get P4 (m/z 236) with the loss of the “-COOH” group. On the other hand, pathway Ⅱ, when CIP is attacked by OH·, defluorination products could be obtained by the C-F bond breaking (Deng et al., 2017), and further decarboxylation to get P5 (m/z 308). The piperazine ring attacked by the oxidative species may result in the product P6 (m/z 282). Finally, the above intermediates yielded weight intermediates P7 (m/z 132) and P8 (m/z 105) and further mineralized to CO2, H2O and inorganic salts. (L. Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020; Yahya et al., 2014).

In this study, the degradation of CIP was investigated in the synergetic system of EBI combined with g-C3N4/CDs. The results suggested that the presence of g-C3N4/CDs could not only promote the degradation of CIP but also significantly enhanced the mineralization ratio. At 5 kGy, the CIP removal rate increased from 63.8% to 77.4% with the g-C3N4/CDs adding dosage of 8 mgmL−1 while the mineralization ratio increased from 18.1% to 69.0% at 25 kGy. Regeneration experiments showed that the degradation efficiency of CIP in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems exceeded 98% within all five cycles, which suggested that the g-C3N4/CDs composite possessed good reusability. In addition to finding that the presence of CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 − and NO2 − obviously restrained the radiolysis process of CIP, the adding of Cl− and SO4 2− could also have slight inhibitory effects. The effect of various water matrices in EBI&g-C3N4/CDs systems showed the best performance in deionized water rather than lake or tap water, which was ascribed to the solutes in the water matrices. Moreover, the studies in the presence of different radical scavengers suggested that ·OH was the main reactive species in the degradation process. Lastly, the degradation pathway of CIP was proposed based on the results of LC-MS and DFT analysis.
Junhui He: Methodology; Writing - original draft; Validation; Investigation; Data curation; Formal analysis.
Qi Ye: Methodology; Writing - original draft; Validation; Investigation; Data curation; Formal analysis.
Yi Zhu: Software; Validation; Formal analysis.
Miao Yang: Project administration; Writing - review & editing; Supervision; Investigation; Methodology.
Long Zhao: Project administration; Writing - review & editing; Supervision; Investigation; Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Advanced Electromagnetic Engineering and Technology (Grant No. AEET 2022KF011) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707108). The authors were grateful to the Analytical and Testing Center of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for providing the facilities to fulfill the experimental measurements. The authors would like to thank the radiation chemistry and functional materials research group of Huazhong University of Science and Technology for the EBI support.
